cues.x: The book is called The Lean Startup: How Today's Entrepreneurs Use Continuous Innovation to Create Radically Successful Businesses, and it's written by Eric Ries.
If you build things for a living, or you dream about building something someday, this is one of those books that quietly rewires how you think.
Not because it hands you a magic formula, but because it destroys a comforting lie you've probably been telling yourself.
The lie goes like this.
If I just work hard enough, if I write a beautiful enough business plan, if I ship a polished enough product, then success will follow.
Eric Ries spent years believing that lie, and watching it fail him and everyone around him.
And what he found on the other side of that failure is what this whole book is about.
So let me walk you through it, slowly and deeply, because there's a lot here, and by the end you're going to look at whatever you're building right now a little differently.
Let's start with the single most important thing Ries does in the entire book, which is to redefine what a startup even is.
Ask most people what a startup is, and they'll picture two kids in a garage, a logo, some venture capital, maybe a foosball table.
Ries throws all of that out.
His definition is this.
A startup is a human institution designed to create something new under conditions of extreme uncertainty.
Sit with that for a second, because every word is doing work.
A human institution, meaning it's about people, about how they organize and coordinate and make decisions.
Designed to create something new, so it's about innovation, not just copying what already works.
And under conditions of extreme uncertainty, which is the part that changes everything.
Notice what's not in that definition.
Nothing about garages.
Nothing about the size of the company.
Nothing about the industry or whether there's a computer involved.
Which means a startup can be two people, and it can also be a team of forty inside a giant corporation trying to launch a new product line.
If you are creating something new and you genuinely do not know whether people will want it or how you'll make it work, you are running a startup, and the ideas in this book apply to you.
That reframing is a gift, because it means you don't need permission or a certain title to think like this.
You just need to be building into the fog.
Now, why should you trust Eric Ries on any of this?
Because he earned these lessons the hard way, through his own failure.
Before he wrote this book, Ries was a co founder and the chief technology officer at a company called IMVU.
And IMVU is where his old worldview died.
The team was brilliant.
They worked insane hours.
They were smart engineers who prided themselves on building things the right way, robust code, elegant architecture, features they were sure customers would love.
They spent about six months building their first product before they ever let a single customer touch it.
Six months of heads down, disciplined, exhausting work.
And when they finally launched, almost nobody used it.
Not because it was buggy, though it was, but because the customers didn't want the thing they'd so carefully built.
Ries describes the gut punch of realizing that all that discipline, all that hard work, had gone into producing something the market simply shrugged at.
And here's the twist that haunted him.
They had done everything they were supposed to do.
They followed the plan.
They executed.
And that's exactly the problem.
He calls this achieving failure, and it's one of the scariest ideas in the book.
Achieving failure means successfully, competently, on time and on budget, executing a plan that leads absolutely nowhere.
You can win every battle and still lose the war, because you were fighting on the wrong continent.
Most failed startups don't fail because the team was lazy or stupid.
They fail because the team did a fantastic job building something nobody needed.
So where did Ries turn to make sense of this?
He looked outside the tech world entirely, to manufacturing, and specifically to Toyota and the lean manufacturing revolution.
This is where the word lean in Lean Startup comes from.
Toyota, over decades, developed a way of making cars that obsessively hunted down and eliminated waste.
In manufacturing, waste is anything that doesn't add value for the customer.
Excess inventory sitting in a warehouse.
Defects that have to be reworked.
Overproduction of parts nobody ordered yet.
The Toyota Production System, built by thinkers like Taiichi Ohno, treated the removal of that waste as a religion.
Ries had a realization.
What if the biggest source of waste in a startup isn't physical?
What if the greatest waste of all is building a feature, or a product, or a whole company, that customers don't want?
All that engineering effort, all those late nights, all that money, poured into something the world was never going to reward.
That's waste on a colossal scale, and nobody was measuring it, because it hides inside activity that looks productive.
The Lean Startup is essentially Ries taking Toyota's war on waste and pointing it at the startup's real enemy, which is uncertainty about what customers actually value.
Which brings us to maybe the most important single concept in the book, and I want you to really hold onto this one.
It's called validated learning.
Ries argues that the fundamental unit of progress for a startup is not a line of code.
It is not a feature shipped.
It is not hours worked or money raised or a beautiful demo.
The unit of progress is validated learning.
Learning that is backed by real evidence from real customers about what they actually do, not what they say in a survey and not what you hope they'll do.
Let me make this concrete with IMVU again.
After the failed launch, Ries and his team could have told themselves a story.
We worked so hard, we shipped so much, we made progress.
But by the only measure that mattered, they'd made almost no progress at all, because they'd learned almost nothing that a customer's behavior had confirmed.
Once he adopted the lens of validated learning, everything looked different.
A week where you ship no code but discover a devastating truth about your customers, a truth that saves you from six wasted months, that's a phenomenally productive week.
And a week where you ship a beautiful new feature that no customer ever uses, that's a week of waste, no matter how good it feels.
This is the mental shift.
Stop asking, can this product be built, because in our era almost anything can be built.
Start asking, should this product be built, and can we build a sustainable business around it.
Those questions can only be answered by learning, and learning only counts when it's validated by evidence.
So how do you generate validated learning on purpose, systematically, instead of by lucky accident?
This is the engine at the heart of the book, and it's called the Build Measure Learn feedback loop.
The idea is that a startup is fundamentally a machine that turns ideas into products, measures how customers respond, and learns whether to pivot or persevere.
You have an idea.
You build a product to test it.
You measure what happens when customers meet that product.
You learn from the data.
And then that learning feeds your next idea, and you go around again.
Build, measure, learn.
Build, measure, learn.
As fast as you possibly can.
And here's the crucial insight that most people miss when they first hear this.
Although the loop runs build, then measure, then learn, you plan it in reverse.
You start at the end.
You first figure out what you need to learn.
What is the single most important thing you're uncertain about right now?
Then you work backwards.
Given what I need to learn, what would I have to measure to know the answer?
And only then, given what I need to measure, what is the smallest, cheapest thing I could build to generate that measurement?
Most engineers, Ries included in his early days, do it the opposite way.
They start with build.
They build the most impressive thing they can, then afterwards try to figure out what to measure, and then hope some learning falls out.
That's backwards, and it's how you end up polishing features nobody asked for.
Design the loop in reverse.
Learn, measure, build, so that you execute it forwards, build, measure, learn, with a clear question you're actually trying to answer.
Now we arrive at the tool that made this book famous, the one term everyone has heard even if they've never read a page.
The Minimum Viable Product, the MVP.
And I want to correct a misunderstanding right away, because people butcher this concept constantly.
The MVP is not a smaller, cheaper version of your final product.
It is not a crappy product.
The MVP is the version of your product that allows you to start the Build Measure Learn loop with the least possible effort, the fastest possible way to get through one full turn of learning.
Its entire job is to test your riskiest assumptions and generate validated learning, nothing more.
It might have terrible design.
It might be held together with tape.
It might not scale to two customers, let alone two million.
That's all fine, because its purpose is not to be your business.
Its purpose is to teach you whether your business is worth building at all.

And the genius of the MVP is that it can take wildly different forms depending on what you need to learn, so let me walk you through the main flavors Ries describes, because they're clever and they'll expand your imagination about what building even means.
First there's the concierge MVP.
The word concierge is the key.
Instead of building software or an automated system, you manually, personally deliver the service to a tiny handful of customers, by hand, like a hotel concierge attending to one guest.
Ries tells the story of a company called Food on the Table, which eventually built software to help families plan meals and grocery lists based on what was on sale at their local stores.
But they did not start by building that software.
The founder started with a single customer.
He personally met with her, personally learned her family's tastes, personally went through the grocery circulars, and personally hand made her a meal plan and shopping list every single week.
One human being serving one other human being.
It was completely un scalable, and that was the point.
He was learning, in intimate detail, exactly what value looked like for a real customer, before writing a single line of the code that would eventually automate it.
Only once he deeply understood one customer did he take on a second, and a third, and only when doing it by hand became genuinely painful did he start building the software to replace himself.
The concierge MVP lets you validate that people actually want the value you're offering before you spend a fortune building the machine to deliver it at scale.
Second, and closely related, there's the Wizard of Oz MVP.
Remember the Wizard of Oz, the great and powerful wizard who turns out to be an ordinary man behind a curtain pulling levers?
That's exactly the trick.
In a Wizard of Oz MVP, the customer thinks they're interacting with a real, automated, finished product.
But behind the curtain, there is no automation at all.
There are humans doing the work by hand, faking the backend.
The customer sees a slick interface and gets results, and has no idea that a person is frantically doing the computation manually on the other side.
Why do this?
Because building the real automated backend is enormously expensive, and you have no idea yet whether customers even want the output.
So you fake it.
You put up the front end, you have humans pretend to be the algorithm, and you watch whether customers engage.
If they do, great, now you know it's worth building the real engine.
If they don't, you've saved yourself from building a sophisticated system nobody wanted.
It's the ultimate way to test demand before you've invested in supply.
Third, there's the video MVP, and the classic example here is Dropbox.
When Drew Houston and his team were building Dropbox, they faced a brutal problem.
Building file syncing that actually works seamlessly across every device is fiendishly hard engineering.
It would take a long time and a lot of money, and they had no proof anyone wanted it.
Worse, it's the kind of product that's hard to demo in a screenshot because the magic is in how smoothly it works over time.
So instead of finishing the product first, they made a simple video.
A three minute screencast showing how Dropbox would work, narrated with little inside jokes aimed at the tech community they wanted to reach.
The product in the video was not fully real.
But the demonstration was compelling enough that it drove their beta waiting list from a few thousand people to around seventy five thousand people overnight.
That's a staggering amount of validated learning about demand, generated by a video, before the hardest engineering was ever finished.
The video was the MVP.
It tested the leap of faith assumption, do people even want this, at almost zero cost compared to shipping the full thing.
Fourth, there's the single feature product or the landing page test.
Sometimes the smallest possible MVP is just a web page that describes your offering and a button that says buy now or sign up.
When people click, maybe they get a message saying you're not quite ready yet, or maybe you fulfill the order manually behind the scenes.
But the click itself is the data.
It's a real person, taking a real action, indicating real intent.
That's worlds more valuable than someone nodding politely and saying, sure, I'd probably use that.
And this connects to one of the most beloved startup stories of all, Zappos.
Nick Swinmurn had a hypothesis, that people would be willing to buy shoes online.
Now, he could have raised a mountain of money, built warehouses, bought inventory, built a sophisticated website, and bet the whole thing on his hunch.
Instead, he did something almost embarrassingly simple.
He went to local shoe stores, and he asked if he could photograph their shoes.
He put those photos up on a bare bones website.
And when someone actually placed an order, he went back to the store, bought the shoes at full retail price, and shipped them himself.
He made no profit.
He built no warehouse.
He automated nothing.
But he learned the one thing that mattered more than anything else.
Real people would really pay real money to buy shoes online, sight unseen.
That single validated fact was the seed of a company that eventually sold to Amazon for over a billion dollars.
He tested the riskiest assumption first, cheaply, with real behavior, before building anything real.
That phrase, riskiest assumption first, deserves its own moment, because Ries gives it a memorable name.
Leap of faith assumptions.
Every business plan, no matter how detailed, rests on a small number of beliefs that, if they turn out to be false, sink the entire thing.
These are the leaps of faith, the things you're betting on without proof.
Ries says there are two that matter most, and you should be able to name yours out loud.
The first is the value hypothesis.
This asks, does my product actually deliver value to customers once they use it?
Do they get something real out of it, something that keeps them coming back?
The second is the growth hypothesis.
This asks, how will new customers discover and adopt my product?
How does word spread, how does the thing grow?
Notice these are separate questions.
A product can deliver enormous value to the few people who use it, and yet have no viable way to reach more of them, and that's a dead end.
Or a product can spread like wildfire and yet deliver so little real value that everyone abandons it, and that's a different dead end.
You have to test both.
And crucially, you test the riskiest one first.
Figure out which assumption, if wrong, would kill you fastest, and design your very first MVP to interrogate that assumption.
Don't waste your first experiment on something you're already fairly confident about.
Aim it at the scariest unknown, the one you've been secretly avoiding because you're afraid of the answer.
And to answer these questions, you cannot sit in your office.
This is where Ries borrows another beautiful idea from Toyota, a Japanese phrase, genchi genbutsu.
It translates roughly as go and see for yourself.
At Toyota, managers were trained never to trust reports and summaries about a problem on the factory floor.
They were expected to physically go to the place where the work happened, to see the actual situation with their own eyes, to touch the actual parts.
You cannot understand a problem from behind a desk.
In startup terms, this becomes the rallying cry, get out of the building.
Your customers are not inside your office.
The truth about your product is not in your own head, and it's definitely not in a spreadsheet of your assumptions.
It's out there, in the messy real world, in the lives and behaviors and frustrations of actual human beings.
So go talk to them.
Watch them.
Sit with them while they try to use your thing and struggle.
Every founder is tempted to hide inside the building, endlessly refining the product, because it feels safe and productive and you get to avoid the terror of a customer telling you your baby is ugly.
Resist that temptation.
The single richest source of validated learning is the customer you're too scared to go talk to.
Genchi genbutsu.
Go and see.
Okay, so you're building MVPs, you're getting out of the building, you're collecting data.
But here's a trap, and it's a subtle one.
How do you know if you're actually making progress?
Because raw numbers can lie to you, beautifully and comfortingly.
This is where Ries introduces a concept he clearly cares deeply about, innovation accounting, and its evil twin, vanity metrics.
Let me start with vanity metrics, because you have almost certainly been fooled by them.
Vanity metrics are numbers that make you feel wonderful but tell you nothing about whether your business actually works.
Total registered users.
Total downloads.
Total page views.
The classic hallmark of a vanity metric is that it's a gross cumulative total, and it only ever goes up and to the right.
Of course your total number of registered users goes up over time, you keep adding new ones and the old ones stay in the count forever, even the ones who signed up once and never came back.
A chart that always climbs feels like success, and that feeling is a drug.
You show it to investors, you show it to your team, everyone feels great, and meanwhile the actual health of your business could be rotting underneath.
Ries lived this at IMVU.
Their gross numbers were growing, and it felt like progress, but when they dug into what individual cohorts of customers were actually doing, the truth was ugly.
The antidote is actionable metrics, and Ries gives you a simple test for whether a metric is worth trusting.
He calls them the three A's.
A metric should be actionable, accessible, and auditable.
Actionable means it demonstrates clear cause and effect.
If I change something and the metric moves, I know my action caused it, so I know what to do next.
A gross total fails this, because when it goes up you can't tell if it's because your product got better or just because time passed.
Accessible means the people who need to act on the number can actually understand it.
Not buried in a data warehouse, not written in impenetrable jargon, but presented in plain terms that everyone on the team can grasp, ideally in terms of real people rather than abstract hits.
And auditable means the data is credible, that you can drill into it and confirm it reflects reality, that you could go talk to the actual customers behind the numbers and the story would hold up.
Metrics that pass all three, actionable, accessible, auditable, are the ones you steer by.
Everything else is decoration.
Two techniques make metrics honest, and you should burn these into your brain.
The first is cohort analysis.
Instead of looking at cumulative totals, you group customers by when they first arrived, their cohort, and you track how each group behaves independently over time.
So you look at everyone who signed up in January, and you ask, what percentage of them came back, what percentage paid, what percentage referred a friend.
Then you look at the February cohort and ask the same questions.
Now you can see, cleanly, whether the changes you're making are improving the experience for new customers or not.
If the February group behaves better than the January group after you shipped an improvement, that's real, honest progress.
The cumulative total might be climbing the whole time and hiding the fact that each new cohort is actually engaging less.
Cohorts strip away that illusion.
The second technique is split testing, also called A B testing.
You offer two different versions of your product to two different groups of customers at the same time, and you measure which one produces better behavior.
This is the cleanest way to establish cause and effect, because everything else is held constant.
Ries notes that split testing often reveals uncomfortable truths, like the feature your engineers spent a month lovingly building makes no measurable difference at all, or even makes things worse.
That's painful, but it's also a gift, because now you know, and you can stop pouring effort down that hole.
So now put innovation accounting together as a three step process, because this is the machinery that tells you whether your engine is working.
Step one, establish the baseline.
Use an MVP to get real data on where you actually stand right now.
What's your real conversion rate, your real retention, your real referral rate, with real customers.
Not your hopes, your baseline.
Step two, tune the engine.
Now you run experiment after experiment, tweak after tweak, each one aimed at moving those baseline metrics toward the ideal you need to build a sustainable business.
Every change is a hypothesis, and cohort analysis and split testing tell you whether it's working.
You're tuning the engine, trying to move the numbers that actually matter closer to viability.
And step three, the moment of truth, pivot or persevere.
After enough tuning, you look honestly at your progress.
Are your experiments actually moving the needle toward a viable business, or have you hit a wall where no amount of tuning gets you where you need to be?
If you're making real progress toward the ideal, you persevere, you keep going, you keep tuning.
But if you've been tuning and tuning and the engine just won't reach the numbers you need, that's your signal.
It's time to pivot.
Innovation accounting is what turns pivot or persevere from a gut feeling and an emotional agony into a decision grounded in evidence.
Let's talk about the pivot, because it's one of the most important and most misunderstood ideas in the whole book.
A pivot is a structured course correction designed to test a new fundamental hypothesis about the product, the strategy, or the engine of growth.
And here's the phrase that captures its spirit.
A pivot is a change in strategy without a change in vision.
You keep one foot planted firmly on the ground of what you've already learned, all that hard won validated learning, and you take a step in a new direction with the other foot.
You're not throwing everything away and starting from scratch.
You're not giving up on your vision.
You're changing the path you take to get there, informed by everything the market has taught you so far.
Ries is very clear that the pivot is not a failure.
It's a normal, expected, healthy part of building something under extreme uncertainty.
In fact, he argues that the number one reason startups die is that they run out of runway, out of money and time, before they've had enough pivots to find the strategy that actually works.
So the real question isn't whether you'll pivot, it's whether you'll do it soon enough, before you run out of gas.
And the thing that keeps most founders from pivoting when they should is not lack of data.
It's courage, and it's ego.
Pivoting means admitting that the current plan, the plan you've been championing, isn't working, and that's emotionally brutal.
The vanity metrics make it easier to avoid, because as long as some number is going up, you can tell yourself things are fine and delay the hard decision.
And a pivot isn't one single move, it's a whole menu of possibilities.
Ries lays out a rich list of pivot types, and knowing them expands your sense of your options when you're stuck.
There's the zoom in pivot, where a single feature of your product turns out to be the whole product, so you double down on just that one thing and cut the rest.
There's the zoom out pivot, the opposite, where what you thought was the whole product turns out to be just one feature of a much bigger thing you need to build.
There's the customer segment pivot, where your product solves a real problem, but for a different type of customer than the one you originally targeted.

There's the customer need pivot, where you've gotten to know your customers so well that you realize the problem you're solving isn't the one they most desperately need solved, so you keep the customer and change the problem.
There's the platform pivot, switching from being an application to being a platform, or the reverse.
There's the business architecture pivot, moving between high margin, low volume and low margin, high volume models.
There's the value capture pivot, changing how you monetize, how you actually capture some of the value you create.
There's the engine of growth pivot, changing your fundamental growth strategy.
There's the channel pivot, changing how you get the product to customers.
And there's the technology pivot, achieving the same solution through completely different technology, usually to get cheaper or better.
The point of all these labels isn't to memorize them like flashcards.
It's to realize that when things aren't working, you have far more options than just quit or push harder.
Pivoting is a precise instrument, not a panic button.

Now, I mentioned the engine of growth a couple of times, so let's dig into it, because Ries argues that sustainable growth, real growth, not a one time spike from a publicity stunt, comes from one of exactly three engines, and you need to know which one is yours.
The first is the sticky engine of growth.
This engine relies on retaining customers.
The whole game is that customers, once they come, tend to stay for a long time, and they stay because switching away would be painful or because the product keeps earning their loyalty.
With a sticky engine, the metric that matters most is your churn rate, the rate at which customers leave.
If your rate of acquiring new customers exceeds your churn rate, you grow, and the lower you can push churn, the faster you compound.
So you obsess over retention.
Think of a product with your data locked inside it, or one that becomes more valuable the longer you use it.
You don't grow by constantly chasing new people so much as by refusing to let the ones you have leak away.
The second is the viral engine of growth.
Here, growth happens as a natural side effect of customers using the product.
Every user, just by using the thing, brings in more users, person to person, like a virus spreading.
The key here is a number Ries calls the viral coefficient, which is how many new customers each existing customer brings in.
If that number is above one, meaning every customer brings in more than one new customer on average, you get explosive, compounding, exponential growth, the kind that feels like magic.
With a viral engine, you don't necessarily grow by charging customers money in the traditional sense.
In fact, charging can slow the virality.
You grow by ruthlessly optimizing that viral loop, making it faster and more effective for each user to bring in the next.
Think of products that spread because using them inherently exposes other people to them.
And the third is the paid engine of growth.
This one is the most straightforward and, in a way, the most disciplined.
You grow by paying to acquire customers, through advertising, through a sales team, through whatever channel costs money.
And the engine works if, and only if, the math works.
Specifically, you look at how much profit you make from a customer over their entire relationship with you, the lifetime value, and you compare it to how much it costs you to acquire that customer.

If the lifetime value is comfortably greater than the cost to acquire, you have a working paid engine, and you can pour money in one end and reliably get more money out the other, and reinvest the surplus into acquiring even more customers.
If those two numbers cross the wrong way, if it costs more to get a customer than they're worth, no amount of hustle saves you, and the engine is broken.
Ries's crucial point about all three engines is that you should focus on one at a time.
Trying to optimize all three simultaneously creates chaos and muddy metrics.
Pick the engine that fits your business, identify the small handful of numbers that drive it, and tune those relentlessly.
And when that engine sputters out and can no longer drive growth, that might be the moment for an engine of growth pivot.
Now let's talk about the pivot or persevere meeting, because Ries doesn't just leave this as a vague feeling you're supposed to have.
He turns it into a discipline, a scheduled event.

He recommends that every startup hold a regular pivot or persevere meeting, bringing together the product development team and the business leadership, on a recurring cadence, maybe every few weeks or every couple of months.
In that meeting, you look honestly at your innovation accounting.
You look at the baseline, you look at all the experiments you've run since the last meeting, and you ask the brutal question.
Is our tuning actually moving us toward a viable business, or are we just spinning our wheels and feeling busy?
The reason you schedule this in advance is precisely because it's so emotionally hard.
If you leave the decision to whenever it feels right, it will never feel right, because there's always one more experiment to try, one more reason to hope.
By putting it on the calendar, you force yourself to confront reality on a regular basis, and you make the pivot decision a normal part of operating rather than a crisis.
Ries is honest that even with the meeting, teams resist pivoting.
They cling to vanity metrics, they doubt their own data, and they're just plain scared.
But the teams that build the discipline to have these meetings honestly are the ones that pivot in time, before the runway runs out.
Alright, let's shift gears now to the third big section of the book, which is about how you actually build, day to day, once you've committed to a direction.
And this is where Ries brings back the Toyota manufacturing wisdom in a really practical way, starting with the power of small batches.
This might sound like a boring operational detail, but it's genuinely counterintuitive and it changes how you work.
Ries tells a wonderful little story to make the point, a thought experiment he actually ran with his sons, or in some tellings a demonstration involving stuffing envelopes.
Imagine you have a big stack of letters to send out.
Each one needs to be folded, put in an envelope, sealed, and stamped.
There are two ways to do this.
The large batch way, which almost everyone's intuition says is faster, is to first fold every single letter, then stuff every envelope, then seal them all, then stamp them all.
You do one operation across the whole batch before moving to the next.
The small batch way is to completely finish one envelope, fold it, stuff it, seal it, stamp it, before starting the next one, one at a time, all the way through.
Now, your gut screams that the large batch approach must be faster, because you get into a rhythm, you're not switching tasks.
But when you actually race them, the small batch approach usually wins.
Why?
Because the large batch approach hides all sorts of problems.
Maybe the letters don't fit the envelopes, and you don't discover it until you've folded all of them.
Maybe you have to stack and move and re sort the big piles between each stage, and all that handling eats the time you thought you were saving.
In the small batch approach, if the letter doesn't fit the envelope, you find out on envelope number one, and you fix it before you've wasted effort on all the rest.
The lesson translates directly to building products.
Working in small batches means you get feedback faster, you find defects sooner, and you dramatically reduce the risk of building a huge pile of the wrong thing before anyone notices.
When you work in large batches, say you build twenty features before releasing anything, and it turns out customers hate the whole direction, you've wasted the effort of all twenty.
When you work in small batches, releasing one small change at a time, you find out after the first one and you've saved yourself the other nineteen.
This is why Ries and companies like IMVU became evangelists for continuous deployment, the practice of releasing new code to customers many times a day, sometimes dozens of times, in tiny increments.
It sounds terrifying and reckless to a traditional engineer.
How can you possibly maintain quality releasing that often?

But the answer is that small batches, combined with the right safety techniques, actually produce higher quality, because problems surface immediately, while the change that caused them is small and fresh and easy to find and fix.
A bug in a giant batch is a needle in a haystack.
A bug in a tiny batch is right in front of you.
And this brings us to building quality in, rather than trying to inspect it in at the end.
Here Ries borrows two more powerful ideas from the Toyota world.
The first is the andon cord.

On a Toyota assembly line, there is a physical cord that runs along the whole line, and any worker, any single worker, no matter how junior, has the authority and the responsibility to pull that cord the instant they notice a defect or a problem.
Pulling the cord stops the entire production line.
Now think about how radical that is.
One person can halt the whole factory.

The philosophy behind it is that it is far, far cheaper to stop and fix a problem the moment it appears than to let a defect flow down the line and get built into hundreds of finished cars that then have to be recalled and reworked.
You never let a known defect move forward.
You stop, you fix the root cause, and only then do you continue.

In software and startups, this translates into building automated systems that halt a bad release the instant a problem is detected, and into a culture where anyone can and should raise a flag when something's wrong, without fear.
The second Toyota technique, and this one is a genuine gift you can start using tomorrow in any part of your life, is the Five Whys.
The Five Whys is a method for root cause analysis.
When something goes wrong, instead of slapping a band aid on the surface symptom, you ask why it happened.
Then you take that answer and ask why again.
And again, roughly five times, until you drill past the technical symptom and hit the real human or systemic root cause underneath.
Ries gives a specific example from IMVU.
Imagine a new version of the product goes out and suddenly a feature stops working for customers.
Why did the feature stop working?
Because a particular server failed.
Okay, why did the server fail?
Because a subsystem was used in the wrong way.
Why was it used the wrong way?
Because the engineer who used it didn't know the correct way.
Why didn't they know?
Because they were never properly trained.
Why weren't they trained?
Because their manager doesn't believe in training new engineers, because the team is too busy and understaffed.
Look at where you ended up.
You started with a broken server, which sounds like a purely technical problem, and five whys later you've discovered that the real root cause is a management decision about training and staffing.
If you'd only fixed the server, the same class of problem would keep happening forever, in new disguises, because the actual cause was never touched.
The Five Whys forces you to see that most technical problems are really human and process problems wearing a technical mask.
But Ries adds a critical warning here, and it's the difference between this technique working and blowing up in your face.
He says the Five Whys can easily curdle into what he calls the Five Blames.
Instead of a calm investigation into a broken process, the meeting turns into a search for the guilty person.
Whose fault was it?
Who wrote the bad code?
Who forgot to train the engineer?

The moment it becomes about blame, people get defensive, they hide problems, they stop pulling the andon cord because pulling it gets them yelled at, and the whole system of honest improvement collapses.
So Ries insists on a rule.
When something goes wrong, you focus on fixing the process, not punishing the person.
You extend genuine tolerance for first time mistakes, and you make it safe to surface problems.
The other principle he attaches to the Five Whys is proportional investment.
When you find the root causes at each of the five levels, you invest in a fix at each level proportional to the size of the problem.
You don't halt the entire company for six months over a small hiccup, and you don't slap a trivial band aid on a catastrophe.

You make a proportional, sensible investment in prevention at every level, so that over time, as you keep encountering small problems and fixing their roots proportionally, your whole system gradually becomes more and more robust, almost automatically.
The Five Whys, done right, is like an automatic speed regulator for how much you invest in quality and infrastructure.
It ties your investment directly to the problems you're actually experiencing, instead of over building infrastructure you don't need yet or under building and drowning in fires.
All of this feeds into the final big idea, which is building an adaptive organization, one that grows at the right speed.
Ries is careful here, because it's easy to hear lean and small batches and think he's saying go as fast as humanly possible, cut every corner, move recklessly.
That's not it at all.
He's saying go the right speed, which means growing your processes and your quality investments in step with your actual needs, not too slow and not too fast.
A startup that invests in zero process and zero quality will eventually get buried in chaos and technical debt and firefighting, and it'll grind to a halt.

But a startup that over invests, that builds heavy bureaucratic processes and gold plated infrastructure before it has the customers to justify them, is just achieving failure in a different costume, wasting time and money on machinery for a business that doesn't exist yet.
The adaptive organization uses tools like the Five Whys to tune its own speed, adding just enough process, just in time, in proportion to the real problems it's hitting.
It stays lean, it stays fast, but it builds in the discipline to not fall apart as it grows.
It's a living system that adjusts itself.
Let me now zoom all the way back out and pull the threads together, because when you stack these ideas up, they form one coherent worldview, and I want you to carry the shape of it with you.
It starts with humility about uncertainty.
You admit you don't actually know what customers want, no matter how sure you feel, because a startup by definition operates under extreme uncertainty.
From that humility flows the goal, which is not to execute a plan flawlessly, because executing a plan flawlessly can lead you straight into achieving failure.
The goal is validated learning, discovering what actually works through real evidence from real customer behavior.
You pursue that learning through the Build Measure Learn loop, which you design in reverse, starting from what you need to learn.
You accelerate the loop with MVPs, the concierge, the Wizard of Oz, the video like Dropbox, the landing page like Zappos, whatever teaches you the most with the least effort.
You aim those experiments at your riskiest leap of faith assumptions, your value hypothesis and your growth hypothesis.
You get out of the building to gather the truth, genchi genbutsu.
You measure with honest, actionable metrics using cohort analysis and split testing, and you refuse to be seduced by vanity metrics that only go up.
You keep the books with innovation accounting, establishing a baseline, tuning the engine, and then deciding, in a scheduled and courageous meeting, whether to pivot or persevere.
When you pivot, you change strategy while keeping your vision, and you have a whole menu of pivot types to choose from.
You grow through one clear engine, sticky, viral, or paid, and you tune the metrics that drive it.
And you build all of it with small batches, continuous deployment, quality built in through the andon cord, and root cause discipline through the Five Whys done with compassion instead of blame.
That's the whole machine.
That's the Lean Startup.
And I want to be honest with you about what this book is not, because Ries himself is careful about this.
The Lean Startup is not a guarantee.
It doesn't promise that if you follow these steps you'll definitely succeed.
Plenty of ideas, tested rigorously and honestly, turn out to be bad ideas, and the lean approach will tell you so, sometimes painfully.
But that's exactly the value.
This method doesn't promise you success.

It promises you the fastest, cheapest possible path to the truth about your idea, so that if it's going to fail, it fails fast and cheap, freeing you to pivot toward something that works, instead of failing slowly and expensively after years of achieving failure.
In a world of extreme uncertainty, that's the best any of us can ask for.
Not certainty, but a reliable way to learn faster than the money runs out.
So let me leave you with something concrete, because ideas are worthless until they touch your actual work.
Here is what you can do this week, starting now, whatever you're building.
First, write down your leap of faith assumptions.
Take ten minutes and honestly name them.
What has to be true for your idea to work?
What's your value hypothesis, the belief that customers will get real value from this?
What's your growth hypothesis, the belief about how you'll actually reach more of them?
Get them out of your head and onto paper where you can look at them.
Second, circle the single riskiest one, the assumption that, if it's false, kills everything, the one you've secretly been avoiding testing because you're afraid of the answer.
That's your target.
Third, design the smallest possible experiment to test that one assumption.
Don't build the product.
Ask, what's my Zappos move here, my Dropbox video, my concierge service?
What is the least I could build or do to get one real signal from one real customer about that riskiest assumption?
Fourth, get out of the building.
This week, actually talk to five real potential customers.
Not friends being nice, not a survey, real conversations where you watch and listen and resist the urge to pitch.
Go and see for yourself.
Fifth, pick one honest metric to watch, one that's actionable, accessible, and auditable, and start tracking it by cohort instead of as a grand cumulative total that only ever climbs.
And sixth, the next time something breaks, anything, resist the urge to just patch the surface.
Sit down and ask why five times, calmly, with no blame, and fix the root cause you find at the bottom.
Do those things, and you'll have run one real turn of the Build Measure Learn loop.
You'll have generated a little validated learning, which is the only real progress there is.
And you'll have started to trade the comfortable lie, that hard work and a good plan guarantee success, for something much more powerful and much more honest.
The willingness to find out what's actually true, faster than anyone else, and to have the courage to change course when the truth demands it.
That's what it means to be a lean entrepreneur.
That's the gift Eric Ries paid for with his own failure at IMVU and handed to you in this book.
Now go build something, measure it honestly, and learn.
The book, one more time, is The Lean Startup by Eric Ries.
Go put it to work.
